---
title: "Bioconductor: software for orchestrating high-throughput genomics data analysis"
author: |
  <br/>
  Sean Davis, MD, PhD<br/>
  Center for Cancer Research</br>
  National Cancer Institute</br>
  <a href="https://twitter.com/seandavis12">@seandavis12</a><br/>
  <a href="https://seandavi.github.io">https://seandavi.github.io</a>
date: "December 4, 2018"
output: 
  revealjs::revealjs_presentation:
    theme: night
    highlight: pygments
    center: false # do not center vertically
    reveal_options:
      slideNumber: true
      previewLinks: true
---
<style type="text/css">
  h1.title {
    text-align: left;
    font-style: italic;
  }
  .reveal .author,.reveal .date {
    text-align: left;
    text-size: 0.8em;
    margin-left: 100px;
  }
  p {
    text-align: left;
  }
  .reveal ul, ol {
    display: block;
    padding-left: 30px;
  }
  .reveal img {
     display: block;
     align: center;
  }
  blockquote p {
    text-align: center;
  }
  #left {
  left:-8.33%;
  text-align: left;
  float: left;
  width:50%;
  z-index:-10;
}

    #wrap { width: 1000px; height: 600px; padding: 0; overflow: hidden; }
    #frame { width: 1000px; height: 600px; border: 1px solid black; }

#right {
  left:31.25%;
  top: 75px;
  float: right;
  text-align: right;
  z-index:-10;
  width:50%;
}
</style>

## Overview

Background and introduction

Two example workflows

- Principle components analysis of public gene expression data
- Somatic variant analysis of TCGA melanoma data

Additional functionality

<br/>
<br/>
<br/>
<br/>

> These slides were written in R and all plots and results are generated from code in the slides--reproducible research in action!

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = FALSE, warning=FALSE, message=FALSE)
```

## Introduction

<div id="left">

![img](http://bioconductor.org/images/logo/jpg/bioconductor_logo_rgb.jpg)
![](https://s3.amazonaws.com/dev.assets.neo4j.com/wp-content/uploads/data-science-efficiency-neo4j-and-r.png)

</div>

<div id="right">
Analysis and comprehension of high-throughput biological data

- Started in 2002
- Builds on [R], which includes 14,376 "contributed" software packages and more than 2 million users worldwide

Bioconductor is well-used and well-respected

- More than 50,000 unique downloads per month
- Approximately 21,700 PubmedCentral citations

</div>

## But what *is* Bioconductor?

> Bioconductor is a Toolkit for high-throughput biological data science built with reproducible computational research as a *core value*.

Bioconductor software supports all aspects the biological data science workflow.

- Data import and export
- Data management and accessibility
- Data integration, including with public data
- Analysis and statistics
- High-level visualization
- Efficiency and reproducibility, even at scale

## Bioconductor Packages

```{r biocpkgdata, cache=TRUE}
require(dplyr)
require(yaml)
library(lubridate)
release_dates = yaml.load_file('https://bioconductor.org/config.yaml')$release_dates
releases = names(release_dates)
df = tibble(date = mdy(release_dates), releases=releases)
df = df[-c(1:8),]
df$packages = sapply(df$releases,function(v) {
  return(nrow(available.packages(repos = BiocInstaller::biocinstallRepos(version=as.character(v))['BioCsoft'])))
})
```

```{r biocpkgplot, fig.align="center"}
require(ggplot2)
ggplot(df,aes(x=date,y=packages)) + 
  geom_point() + 
  geom_smooth(method="glm",
             method.args=list(family=gaussian(link="log"))) +
  scale_x_date(date_breaks = "1 year", date_labels = "%Y") + 
  theme(text = element_text(size=16))
```


## The Bioconductor Ecosystem

```{r infrastructure}
library(visNetwork)
nodes = data.frame(label=c('Build System','Website','AnnotationHub','ExperimentHub','ExperimentData','AnnotationData','AnnotationHub','Support Site','Email List','Version Control','Packages', "Community", "Reproducibility", "Science", "IT"),
                   group=c(rep("Component",11),rep("System",4)),
                   size=(c(rep(20,11),40,40,40,40)),
                   id=1:15)
links = data.frame(from=c(1, 1, 2, 2, 3, 3, 3, 4, 4, 4, 5, 5, 6, 6, 7, 7, 7, 8, 8, 9, 9, 10,10,10,11,11,11,11),
                   to=  c(15,14,15,12,15,14,13,15,14,13,14,13,14,13,15,14,13,15,12,15,12,12,13,14,12,13,14,15),length=350)
visNetwork(nodes,links) %>% 
    visEdges(shadow = TRUE,
             arrows =list(to = list(enabled = FALSE, scaleFactor = 2)),
             selectionWidth = 10) %>%
    visNodes(font = list(size=28,color='white'),
             labelHighlightBold = TRUE) %>%
    visOptions(highlightNearest = list(enabled =TRUE))
```

## Bioconductor: Education, Training, and Community

<div id="wrap">
<iframe id="frame" src="https://bioconductor.org/"></iframe>
</div>


## SummarizedExperiment

<div style="width:80%; display: block; margin: 0 auto;">
![](https://images.nature.com/full/nature-assets/nmeth/journal/v12/n2/images/nmeth.3252-F2.jpg)
</div>


## Example 1: Build a PCA plot from Public Data

Use the [GEOquery] package to fetch data about [GSE103512].


```{r geoquery10, echo=TRUE, cache=TRUE}
library(GEOquery)
gse = getGEO("GSE103512", destdir='/tmp')[[1]]
gse
```

## Example 1: Build a PCA plot from Public Data

Examine two variables of interest, cancer type and tumor/normal status.
```{r geoquery20,echo=TRUE,cache=TRUE,results='asis'}
library(xtable)
print(xtable(with(pData(gse),table(`cancer type:ch1`,`normal:ch1`))),type='html')
```

## Example 1: Build a PCA plot from Public Data

Information about features measured are also included.

```{r geoquery30,echo=FALSE,cache=TRUE,results='asis'}
library(DT)
cnames = c('Gene Symbol','Gene Title','ENTREZ_GENE_ID')
df = subset(fData(gse)[,cnames],!grepl('///',`Gene Symbol`))
DT::datatable(df[1:1000,],extensions = 'Scroller',
              options = list(scrollX = TRUE,  
                             deferRender = TRUE,
                             scrollY = 350,
                             scroller = TRUE)) %>% 
    formatStyle(columns = cnames,color='white',background = 'black',target = 'row')
```

## Example 1: Build a PCA plot from Public Data

Filter gene expression by variance to find most informative genes.

```{r sds,cache=TRUE,echo=TRUE}
sds = apply(exprs(gse),1,sd)
dat = exprs(gse)[order(sds,decreasing = TRUE)[1:500],]
```

Perform [multidimensional scaling] and prepare for plotting.

```{r mds,echo=TRUE,cache=TRUE}
mdsvals = cmdscale(dist(t(dat)))
mdsvals = as.data.frame(mdsvals)
mdsvals$Type=factor(pData(gse)[,'cancer type:ch1'])
mdsvals$Normal = factor(pData(gse)[,'normal:ch1'])
```

And do the plot.

```{r mdsplot,echo=TRUE,fig.align='center',fig.show='hide'}
library(ggplot2)
ggplot(mdsvals, aes(x=V1,y=V2,shape=Normal,color=Type)) + 
    geom_point(size=4, alpha=0.6) + theme(text=element_text(size = 18))
```



## Example 1: Build a PCA plot from Public Data

```{r ref.label='mdsplot',echo=FALSE,fig.align='center'}
```

## Example 2: Melanoma Variants from NCI Genomic Data Commons Data

<div style="width:80%; display: block; margin: 0 auto;">
![Imgur](https://i.imgur.com/c9axxjI.png)
</div>

## Example 2: Melanoma Variants from NCI Genomic Data Commons Data

Use the [GenomicDataCommons](https://bioconductor.org/packages/GenomicDataCommons) package to find
and download variants from the TCGA cutaneous melanoma dataset.

```{r gdc_getdata,cache=TRUE, echo=TRUE, results='hide'}
library(GenomicDataCommons)
fnames = files() %>% 
    GenomicDataCommons::filter(~ cases.project.project_id=='TCGA-SKCM' & 
        data_type=='Masked Somatic Mutation' &
        data_format=='MAF' &
        analysis.workflow_type=='MuTect2 Variant Aggregation and Masking') %>%
    ids() %>%
    gdcdata()
```

And now take those data directly to [maftools](https://bioconductor.org/package/maftools) for 
analysis and visualization.

```{r mafsummary, cache=TRUE, echo=TRUE, results='hide'}
library(maftools)
melanoma = read.maf(maf = fnames[1])
```

## Example 2: Melanoma Variants from NCI Genomic Data Commons Data

```{r plotmafsummary,fig.align='center'}
library(maftools)
plotmafSummary(melanoma)
```


## Example 2: Melanoma Variants from NCI Genomic Data Commons Data

```{r oncoplot, fig.align='center'}
library(maftools)
oncoplot(maf=melanoma,top=15,genes = getGeneSummary(melanoma)$Hugo_Symbol[3:20])
```

## Example 2: Melanoma Variants from NCI Genomic Data Commons Data

```{r oncodrive, cache=TRUE, fig.height=5, echo=TRUE, results='hide'}
v = oncodrive(maf = melanoma, AACol = 'HGVSp_Short', minMut = 5, pvalMethod = 'zscore')
plotOncodrive(v,labelSize=40,fdrCutOff = 0.1)
```

## Example 2: Melanoma Variants from NCI Genomic Data Commons Data

```{r lollipop, cache=TRUE}
lollipopPlot(maf = melanoma, gene = 'BRAF', AACol = 'HGVSp_Short', 
             showMutationRate = TRUE, domainLabelSize = 3, defaultYaxis = FALSE)
```

## Other functionality: Ranges

<div style="width:80%; display: block; margin: 0 auto;">
![Imgur](https://i.imgur.com/wYL8bT6.png)
</div> 

## Other functionality: Sequencing

<div style="width:80%; display: block; margin: 0 auto;">
![Imgur](https://i.imgur.com/BTYXWjA.png)
</div>

## Data access and management

Hubs and data packages give users access to curated and versioned public data.

- AnnotationHub
    - Reference genomes
    - Gene models
    - ENCODE data
    - UCSC Tracks
- ExperimentHub
    - Curated and versioned public datasets (TCGA, 1000Genomes)
    - Searchable metadata
- Experiment data packages
    - Easy sharing of data within lab, institute, or internationally

## Reproducible research

- Everything in bioconductor is version controlled and versioned. 
- Package build system guarantees compatibility between packages.
- Documentation and workflows are "literate", so if they do not work or do not match
code, Bioc build system alerts developer.
- Strong reliance on interoperability to reduce "reinvention" (and decrease mistakes)

## Questions

- https://bioconductor.org
- https://support.bioconductor.org
- Package documentation
- Package vignettes (or workflows)
- https://f1000research.com/articles/4-217/v2
- https://www.youtube.com/user/bioconductor


Contact me:

- https://seandavi.github.io/
- https://twitter.com/seandavis12
- mailto:seandavi@gmail.com or mailto:sean.davis@nih.gov




[R]: https://cran.r-project.org/
[GEOquery]: https://bioconductor.org/packages/GEOquery
[GSE103512]: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/geo/query/acc.cgi?acc=GSE103512
[multidimensional scaling]: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Multidimensional_scaling
